inputs.story: Jack has added several new jigsaw puzzles to his sizable collection this year. Determine each puzzle's size (number of pieces), theme, issuing year and company. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
inputs.constraints:
1. The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has somewhat more than the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme.
2. The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces.
3. The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme.
4. The jigsaw puzzle made by Denlend has somewhat fewer than the jigsaw puzzle with the outer space theme.
5. The jigsaw puzzle made by Kimsight has 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend.
inputs.constants:
companies: Buralde; Denlend; Irycia; Kimsight.
pieces: 250; 500; 750; 1000.
themes: autumn leaves; coral reef; outer space; rustic village.
inputs.solution:
Pieces	Companies	Themes
250	Denlend	rustic village
500	Kimsight	autumn leaves
750	Buralde	outer space
1000	Irycia	coral reef

asp_generation.prompt:
<task>
You are given a logic grid puzzle with a story, numbered clues, and categorized constants. Your task is to generate a complete ASP (Answer Set Programming) program that, when solved with Clingo, yields a unique answer set representing the puzzle's solution.

Provided below are:
- two examples: `example_1` and `example_2`,
- the problem you must solve: `problem_to_solve`,
- important rules: `rules`.
</task>

<example_1>
"Against the Grain" offers hand-made wooden furniture at reasonable prices. Each item is made by an in-house employee. Using only the clues that follow, match each item to the employee who crafted it, and determine its price and the type of wood used to make it. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.

Constants:
Employees: Bonita; Yvette; Tabitha.
Prices: $225; $275; $325.
Wood types: ash; poplar; sandalwood.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
employee("Bonita"; "Yvette"; "Tabitha").
price(225; 275; 325).
wood_type("ash"; "poplar"; "sandalwood").

% Search space: for each employee E, assign exactly one price P and wood type W.
{match(E, P, W): price(P), wood_type(W)}=1 :- employee(E).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{E1=E2; P1=P2; W1=W2}=0 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), (E1,P1,W1)!=(E2,P2,W2).

% 1. Bonita's piece costs $325.
P=325 :- match(E,P,W), E="Bonita".

% 2. The item made of poplar costs more than Yvette's piece.
P1>P2 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), W1="poplar", E2="Yvette".

% 3. Tabitha's item costs 50 dollars less than the piece made of sandalwood.
P1=P2-50 :- match(E1,P1,W1), match(E2,P2,W2), E1="Tabitha", W2="sandalwood".

% 4. The $275 item is either the piece made of ash or Yvette's item.
{W="ash"; E="Yvette"}=1 :- match(E,P,W), P=275.

#show match/3.
```
</example_1>

<example_2>
The Winter Olympics have just wrapped up in Norway. Using only the clues that follow, determine the number of gold and silver medals won by each country. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The four teams are the team from Bolivia, the team that won 3 gold medals, the team that won 6 silver medals, and the team from Argentina.
2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams.
5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.

Constants:
Countries: Argentina; Bolivia; Grenada; Oman.
Silver medals: 2; 6; 10; 11.
Gold medals: 1; 2; 3; 4.

ASP Program:
```asp
% Constants
country("Argentina"; "Bolivia"; "Grenada"; "Oman").
silver_medals(2; 6; 10; 11).
gold_medals(1; 2; 3; 4).

% Search space: for each country C, assign exactly one silver medal count S and gold medal count G.
{assign(C, S, G): silver_medals(S), gold_medals(G)}=1 :- country(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; S1=S2; G1=G2}=0 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), (C1,S1,G1)!=(C2,S2,G2).

% 1. The four teams are all different — Bolivia, the 3-gold team, the 6-silver team, and Argentina each refer to distinct countries.
{C="Bolivia"; G=3; S=6; C="Argentina"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G).

% 2. The team from Oman and the team that won 10 silver medals are different.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Oman", S2=10.

% 3. The team from Oman finished with 2 gold medals or finished with 1 gold medal.
{G=2; G=1}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), C="Oman".

% 4. The squad that won 3 gold medals, the squad that won 6 silver medals and the squad from Bolivia were all different teams — three pairwise inequalities.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C2!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".
C1!=C3 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), assign(C3,S3,G3), G1=3, S2=6, C3="Bolivia".

% 5. Neither the team from Argentina nor the squad that won 2 silver medals is the squad that won 4 gold medals.
{C="Argentina"; S=2}=0 :- assign(C,S,G), G=4.
C1!=C2 :- assign(C1,S1,G1), assign(C2,S2,G2), C1="Argentina", S2=2.

% 6. The squad that won 2 gold medals is either the squad that won 6 silver medals or the team from Grenada.
{S=6; C="Grenada"}=1 :- assign(C,S,G), G=2.

#show assign/3.
```
</example_2>

Here is the problem to be solved:
<problem_to_solve>
Jack has added several new jigsaw puzzles to his sizable collection this year. Determine each puzzle's size (number of pieces), theme, issuing year and company. Remember, as with all grid-based logic puzzles, no option in any category will ever be used more than once.
1. The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has somewhat more than the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme.
2. The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces.
3. The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme.
4. The jigsaw puzzle made by Denlend has somewhat fewer than the jigsaw puzzle with the outer space theme.
5. The jigsaw puzzle made by Kimsight has 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend.

Constants:
companies: Buralde; Denlend; Irycia; Kimsight.
pieces: 250; 500; 750; 1000.
themes: autumn leaves; coral reef; outer space; rustic village.
</problem_to_solve>

Here are some important rules to follow when generating the ASP code:
<rules>
1. Constant declarations — "category(const1; const2; ...)."
   - Category names: lowercase with underscores only (e.g., wood_type, gold_medals).
   - All constants in the same category must use the same format — all integers or all quoted strings.
   - Prefer integers: strip special characters ($225 → 225), strip units while keeping the number ("3 days" → 3, "2 months" → 2), and convert verbal or ordinal numerals (once → 1, twice → 2, third → 3, September → 9).
   - Use double-quoted strings when no integer representation is possible (e.g., "Bonita", "9:30AM").

2. Predicates
   - Use the minimum number of predicates needed to relate all categories.
   - Every category must appear in at least one predicate argument; all arguments must be distinct variables.
   - Choose a name that reflects the relationship (e.g., match, assign, schedule, launch).

3. Search space — one choice rule per predicate, anchoring on one category in the body and ranging over the rest inside the braces:
     {pred(V1, V2, ...): cat2(V2), cat3(V3), ...}=1 :- cat1(V1).

4. Uniqueness — no value in any non-anchor category appears more than once:
     {V1=V2; W1=W2; ...}=0 :- pred(V1,W1,...), pred(V2,W2,...), (V1,W1,...)!=(V2,W2,...).

5. Constraint rules — encode each clue using one or more rules of these forms, using only defined constants and predicates:
     <C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.      % if all Li hold, at least one Ci must hold
     {<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.  % if all Li hold, exactly k of Ci hold
     :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.                        % this combination is forbidden

   Common patterns:
   - "Of A and B, one is C and the other is D" → three rules: A≠B, A is C or D, B is C or D.
   - "A, B and C are all different" → three pairwise inequality rules (A≠B, A≠C, B≠C).
   - "Neither A nor B is X" → {A_cond; B_cond}=0 :- ... X ... .

6. Show directives — one "#show pred/arity." per predicate.
</rules>

ASP Program:
asp_generation.thinking:
Here's a thinking process:

1.  **Understand the Goal**: The user wants an ASP (Answer Set Programming) program in Clingo format that solves a specific logic grid puzzle. The program must follow the provided examples and rules strictly.

2.  **Analyze the Problem**:
   - **Categories**:
     - Companies: Buralde, Denlend, Irycia, Kimsight
     - Pieces: 250, 500, 750, 1000
     - Themes: autumn leaves, coral reef, outer space, rustic village
   - **Clues**:
     1. The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has somewhat more than the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme. (pieces(autumn) > pieces(rustic))
     2. The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces. (All four are distinct entities. This implies: company(Buralde) != theme(rustic_village), company(Buralde) != theme(autumn_leaves), company(Buralde) != pieces(1000), theme(rustic_village) != theme(autumn_leaves), theme(rustic_village) != pieces(1000), theme(autumn_leaves) != pieces(1000). Actually, it means these four descriptions refer to four *different* puzzles. So we need pairwise inequalities among the puzzles described by these attributes.)
     3. The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme. (company(Buralde) -> theme(outer_space))
     4. The jigsaw puzzle made by Denlend has somewhat fewer than the jigsaw puzzle with the outer space theme. (pieces(Denlend) < pieces(outer_space))
     5. The jigsaw puzzle made by Kimsight has 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend. (pieces(Kimsight) = pieces(Denlend) + 250)

   Let's carefully map the clues to ASP constraints.

   Clue 1: "The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has somewhat more than the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme."
   -> `P1 > P2 :- match(C1,P1,T1), match(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", T2="rustic village".`
   Wait, "somewhat more" usually just means strictly greater in these puzzles. I'll use `P1 > P2`.

   Clue 2: "The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces."
   This means these four refer to four *different* puzzles. So:
   - Buralde != rustic village
   - Buralde != autumn leaves
   - Buralde != 1000 pieces
   - rustic village != autumn leaves
   - rustic village != 1000 pieces
   - autumn leaves != 1000 pieces
   In ASP, we can encode this as pairwise inequalities. Since each attribute belongs to a different category (company, theme, pieces), we need to check if they refer to the same puzzle.
   Actually, the standard way to encode "A, B, C, D are different" in a grid puzzle where each has a distinct attribute value is to say that the puzzle with attribute A cannot have attribute B, etc.
   So:
   `:- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde", T="rustic village".`
   `:- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde", T="autumn leaves".`
   `:- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde", P=1000.`
   `:- match(C,P,T), T="rustic village", T2="autumn leaves" ...` wait, themes are distinct by definition, but the clue says they are different *puzzles*, meaning a puzzle cannot have two themes. But we already enforce uniqueness of themes per puzzle via the search space. The clue means the puzzle identified by Buralde is not the puzzle identified by rustic village, etc.
   So:
   `:- match(C1,P1,T1), match(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", T2="rustic village", C1=C2.` (Actually, simpler: `:- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde", T="rustic village".`)
   Wait, the standard encoding for "The four items are X, Y, Z, W" in these puzzles is that they refer to four distinct rows. So:
   `:- match(C1,P1,T1), match(C2,P2,T2), match(C3,P3,T3), match(C4,P4,T4), C1="Buralde", T2="rustic village", T3="autumn leaves", P4=1000, C1=C2, C1=C3, C1=C4, C2=C3, C2=C4, C3=C4.`
   This is tedious. A simpler way: For each pair, ensure they don't match on the same puzzle.
   Actually, the clue means: The puzzle made by Buralde is NOT the rustic village one, NOT the autumn leaves one, NOT the 1000-piece one. The rustic village one is NOT the autumn leaves one (implied by theme uniqueness), NOT the 1000-piece one. The autumn leaves one is NOT the 1000-piece one.
   So:
   `:- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde", T="rustic village".`
   `:- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde", T="autumn leaves".`
   `:- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde", P=1000.`
   `:- match(C,P,T), T="rustic village", P=1000.`
   `:- match(C,P,T), T="autumn leaves", P=1000.`
   (Theme-theme inequality is implicit since each puzzle has exactly one theme, but we don't need to enforce it explicitly unless needed. The clue just emphasizes they are distinct entities.)

   Clue 3: "The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme."
   -> `T="outer space" :- match(C,P,T), C="Buralde".`

   Clue 4: "The jigsaw puzzle made by Denlend has somewhat fewer than the jigsaw puzzle with the outer space theme."
   -> `P1 < P2 :- match(C1,P1,T1), match(C2,P2,T2), C1="Denlend", T2="outer space".`

   Clue 5: "The jigsaw puzzle made by Kimsight has 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend."
   -> `P1 = P2 + 250 :- match(C1,P1,_), match(C2,P2,_), C1="Kimsight", C2="Denlend".`

3.  **Structure the ASP Program**:
   - Constants: `company(...)`, `pieces(...)`, `theme(...)`
   - Predicate: `puzzle(C, P, T)` or `assign(C, P, T)` or `match(C, P, T)`. I'll use `puzzle(C, P, T)`.
   - Search space: `{puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), theme(T)}=1 :- company(C).`
   - Uniqueness: `{C1=C2; P1=P2; T1=T2}=0 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), (C1,P1,T1)!=(C2,P2,T2).`
   - Constraints based on clues.

   Let's draft it carefully.

   ```asp
   % Constants
   company("Buralde"; "Denlend"; "Irycia"; "Kimsight").
   pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).
   theme("autumn leaves"; "coral reef"; "outer space"; "rustic village").

   % Search space
   {puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), theme(T)}=1 :- company(C).

   % Uniqueness
   {C1=C2; P1=P2; T1=T2}=0 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), (C1,P1,T1)!=(C2,P2,T2).

   % Clue 1: autumn leaves theme has more pieces than rustic village theme
   P1>P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", T2="rustic village".

   % Clue 2: The four puzzles are distinct (Buralde, rustic village, autumn leaves, 1000 pieces)
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", T2="rustic village", C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", T2="autumn leaves", C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", P2=1000, C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="rustic village", P2=1000, C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", P2=1000, C1=C2.

   % Clue 3: Buralde has outer space theme
   T="outer space" :- puzzle(C,P,T), C="Buralde".

   % Clue 4: Denlend has fewer pieces than outer space theme
   P1<P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Denlend", T2="outer space".

   % Clue 5: Kimsight has 250 more pieces than Denlend
   P1=P2+250 :- puzzle(C1,P1,_), puzzle(C2,P2,_), C1="Kimsight", C2="Denlend".

   #show puzzle/3.
   ```

4.  **Check against Rules**:
   - Rule 1: Constants format. `pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).` (integers) `company("Buralde"; ...)` (strings) `theme("autumn leaves"; ...)` (strings). Good.
   - Rule 2: Predicates. `puzzle(C, P, T)` relates all categories. Arguments distinct. Good.
   - Rule 3: Search space. `{puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), theme(T)}=1 :- company(C).` Anchors on company, ranges over pieces and theme. Good.
   - Rule 4: Uniqueness. `{C1=C2; P1=P2; T1=T2}=0 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), (C1,P1,T1)!=(C2,P2,T2).` Good.
   - Rule 5: Constraints. Used `<C1>; ...` or `{...}=k` or `:- ...`. I used `:- ...` for inequalities and `P1>P2 :- ...` which is actually a rule, not a constraint. Wait, the rule says: `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.` So `P1>P2 :- ...` is valid. It means if the body holds, the head must hold. That's correct.
   - Let's verify Clue 2 encoding. The clue says "The four puzzles are X, Y, Z, W." This means they are four distinct entities. My encoding ensures no two descriptions apply to the same puzzle. That's correct.
   - Wait, is `P1>P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", T2="rustic village".` correct? Yes, it forces the puzzle with autumn leaves to have more pieces than the puzzle with rustic village.
   - Clue 4: `P1<P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Denlend", T2="outer space".` Correct.
   - Clue 5: `P1=P2+250 :- puzzle(C1,P1,_), puzzle(C2,P2,_), C1="Kimsight", C2="Denlend".` Correct.
   - Check Clue 3: `T="outer space" :- puzzle(C,P,T), C="Buralde".` Correct.
   - Check Clue 2 again: "The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces."
     This implies pairwise distinctness. My constraints:
     `:- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", T2="rustic village", C1=C2.`
     This says: It's forbidden for the same puzzle to be both Buralde and rustic village. Correct.
     Similarly for others. This covers all pairs. Good.

   One minor point: In ASP, `P1>P2 :- ...` is fine, but sometimes it's better to write it as a constraint if it's an inequality? No, the rule format `<C1>; ...` allows `P1>P2` as a head. Actually, `P1>P2 :- Body.` is a valid rule. It says "If Body, then P1 must be > P2". That's correct.
   Alternatively, I could use `:- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", T2="rustic village", P1=<P2.` but the prompt says to use `<C1>; ...` or `{...}=k` or `:- ...`. I'll stick with `P1>P2 :- ...`.

   Let's double check the exact wording of the prompt for rules:
   `<C1>; ...; <Cm> :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `{<C1>; ...; <Cm>}=k :- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   `:- <L1>, ..., <Ln>.`
   So `P1>P2 :- ...` fits the first form.

   Wait, is there any issue with `P1=P2+250`? It's an arithmetic atom in the head. Clingo supports this. It's fine.

   Let's test the logic mentally:
   Companies: Buralde, Denlend, Irycia, Kimsight.
   Pieces: 250, 500, 750, 1000.
   Themes: autumn leaves, coral reef, outer space, rustic village.

   From Clue 3: Buralde -> outer space.
   From Clue 4: Denlend < outer space. So Denlend < Buralde.
   From Clue 5: Kimsight = Denlend + 250.
   Pieces available: 250, 500, 750, 1000.
   If Denlend = 250, Kimsight = 500. Then Buralde > 500 (from Clue 4), so Buralde could be 750 or 1000. Irycia gets the rest.
   If Denlend = 500, Kimsight = 750. Buralde > 750 -> 1000. Irycia = 250.
   If Denlend = 750, Kimsight = 1000. Buralde > 1000 -> impossible.
   So Denlend is either 250 or 500.

   Clue 1: autumn leaves > rustic village.
   Clue 2: Buralde, rustic village, autumn leaves, 1000 pieces are all different.
   We know Buralde has outer space theme. So Buralde != rustic village, Buralde != autumn leaves. (Already covered)
   Buralde != 1000 pieces. So Buralde's pieces != 1000.
   From above, if Denlend=500, Buralde=1000. But Buralde != 1000. So Denlend cannot be 500.
   Therefore, Denlend=250, Kimsight=500.
   Buralde > 500 and Buralde != 1000 -> Buralde=750.
   Irycia=1000.
   So pieces: Denlend=250, Kimsight=500, Buralde=750, Irycia=1000.
   Themes: Buralde=outer space.
   Clue 2 says: Buralde(750), rustic village, autumn leaves, 1000 pieces(Irycia) are all different.
   So Irycia != rustic village, Irycia != autumn leaves. Irycia must be coral reef.
   So themes: Buralde=outer space, Irycia=coral reef.
   Remaining themes for Denlend and Kimsight: rustic village, autumn leaves.
   Clue 1: autumn leaves > rustic village.
   Denlend=250, Kimsight=500. So autumn leaves=500 (Kimsight), rustic village=250 (Denlend).
   Check Clue 4: Denlend(250) < outer space(750). True.
   Check Clue 5: Kimsight(500) = Denlend(250) + 250. True.
   Solution:
   Buralde: 750, outer space
   Denlend: 250, rustic village
   Irycia: 1000, coral reef
   Kimsight: 500, autumn leaves
   This is unique and consistent.

   The ASP program should find this unique solution.

   Let's refine the ASP code to match the examples exactly.
   I will use `puzzle(C, P, T)` as the predicate.
   I'll format constants exactly as requested.
   I'll ensure all rules follow the specified patterns.

   One detail: Clue 2 says "The four puzzles are...". In logic grid puzzles, this usually means they are four distinct rows. My constraints enforce that no two descriptions apply to the same puzzle. That's standard.
   I will write out the constraints explicitly as `:- ...` rules.

   Let's draft carefully:

   ```asp
   % Constants
   company("Buralde"; "Denlend"; "Irycia"; "Kimsight").
   pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).
   theme("autumn leaves"; "coral reef"; "outer space"; "rustic village").

   % Search space: for each company C, assign exactly one piece count P and theme T.
   {puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), theme(T)}=1 :- company(C).

   % No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
   {C1=C2; P1=P2; T1=T2}=0 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), (C1,P1,T1)!=(C2,P2,T2).

   % 1. The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has somewhat more than the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme.
   P1>P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", T2="rustic village".

   % 2. The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", T2="rustic village", C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", T2="autumn leaves", C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Buralde", P2=1000, C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="rustic village", P2=1000, C1=C2.
   :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", P2=1000, C1=C2.

   % 3. The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme.
   ```

   ```asp
company("Buralde"; "Denlend"; "Irycia"; "Kimsight").
pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).
theme("autumn leaves"; "coral reef"; "outer space"; "rustic village").

% Search space: for each company C, assign exactly one piece count P and theme T.
{puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), theme(T)}=1 :- company(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; P1=P2; T1=T2}=0 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), (C1,P1,T1)!=(C2,P2,T2).

% 1. The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has somewhat more than the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme.
P1>P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", T2="rustic village".

% 2. The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces.
{C="Buralde"; T="rustic village"; T="autumn leaves"; P=1000}=1 :- puzzle(C,P,T).

% 3. The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme.
T="outer space" :- puzzle(C,P,T), C="Buralde".

P1<P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Denlend", T2="outer space".

% 5. The jigsaw puzzle made by Kimsight has 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend.
P1=P2+250 :- puzzle(C1,P1,_), puzzle(C2,P2,_), C1="Kimsight", C2="Denlend".

#show puzzle/3.
   ```

   ```asp
% Constants
company("Buralde"; "Denlend"; "Irycia"; "Kimsight").
pieces(250; 500; 750; 1000).
theme("autumn leaves"; "coral reef"; "outer space"; "rustic village").

% Search space: for each company C, assign exactly one piece count P and theme T.
{puzzle(C, P, T): pieces(P), theme(T)}=1 :- company(C).

% No option in any category will ever be used more than once.
{C1=C2; P1=P2; T1=T2}=0 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), (C1,P1,T1)!=(C2,P2,T2).

% 1. The puzzle with the autumn leaves theme has somewhat more than the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme.
P1>P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), T1="autumn leaves", T2="rustic village".

% 2. The four puzzles are the puzzle made by Buralde, the jigsaw puzzle with the rustic village theme, the puzzle with the autumn leaves theme and the puzzle with 1000 pieces.
{C="Buralde"; T="rustic village"; T="autumn leaves"; P=1000}=1 :- puzzle(C,P,T).

% 3. The jigsaw puzzle made by Buralde has the outer space theme.
T="outer space" :- puzzle(C,P,T), C="Buralde".

% 4. The jigsaw puzzle made by Denlend has somewhat fewer than the jigsaw puzzle with the outer space theme.
P1<P2 :- puzzle(C1,P1,T1), puzzle(C2,P2,T2), C1="Denlend", T2="outer space".

% 5. The jigsaw puzzle made by Kimsight has 250 more pieces than the puzzle made by Denlend.
P1=P2+250 :- puzzle(C1,P1,_), puzzle(C2,P2,_), C1="Kimsight", C2="Denlend".

#show puzzle/3.
   ```
